title: Telecommunications, Media, and Net Neutrality: Political Spending and Influence

sector_overview.total_since_1998: $1.84B in telecom services lobbying (OpenSecrets, 12th biggest industry)
sector_overview.116th_congress_total: $234M by top 15 ISPs and trade associations (2019-2020)

t_mobile.junk_fee_lobbying: $6.8M since Q3 2023 lobbying against Junk Fee Prevention Act

ctia.rank: 9th overall among all lobbying spenders in 2023

ncta.ceo: Michael Powell (former FCC Chairman, CEO since April 2011)

ustelecom.position_on_acp: Publicly supported extension of Affordable Connectivity Program
ustelecom.bead_stance: Engaged with NTIA and state broadband leaders on infrastructure law implementation

telecom_lobbying_totals.combined_big_four_2023: $117M in federal lobbying (AT&T, Comcast, Charter, Verizon combined) plus $25M+ in state-level lobbying

timeline.2010_open_internet_order: FCC adopted limited net neutrality rules; struck down by DC Circuit in Verizon v. FCC (January 2014)
timeline.2015_open_internet_order: FCC voted February 26, 2015 to reclassify broadband as Title II telecommunications service; released March 12, 2015
timeline.2017_pai_repeal: FCC voted December 14, 2017 to reverse 2015 rules via 'Restoring Internet Freedom' order; NPRM began May 18, 2017
timeline.2024_fcc_restoration: FCC issued Safeguarding and Securing the Open Internet order May 7, 2024; effective July 22, 2024
timeline.2024_sixth_circuit_stay: Administrative stay July 12, 2024; full stay August 1, 2024 citing Loper Bright
timeline.2025_sixth_circuit_ruling: January 2, 2025 — unanimous decision striking down 2024 order entirely in Ohio Telecom Ass'n v. FCC
timeline.legal_basis: Court held broadband is 'information service' not 'telecommunications service' and FCC exceeded statutory authority

lobbying_spending.2008_to_2017_total: $572M by Comcast, AT&T, Verizon, and NCTA to influence FCC and other agencies
lobbying_spending.2017_repeal_support: $110M by telecom industry supporting net neutrality repeal
lobbying_spending.per_comment_ratio: For every 1 public comment on net neutrality, ISPs spent $100 on lobbying (MapLight analysis)

net_neutrality_battles.loper_bright_impact: Supreme Court's June 2024 Loper Bright decision (overturning Chevron deference) became basis for striking down FCC net neutrality rules — first major application of the ruling

tom_wheeler.pre_fcc: President/CEO of NCTA (1979-1984); CEO of CTIA (1992-2004)
tom_wheeler.appointed_by: Obama (bundled $700,000+ for Obama campaigns in 2007 and 2012)
tom_wheeler.notable: Despite industry background, championed net neutrality (February 2015 Title II reclassification)

ajit_pai.pre_fcc: Attorney at Jenner & Block (represented Securus prison phone company); DOJ and FCC staff
ajit_pai.key_action: Repealed net neutrality December 14, 2017; dropped prison phone rate limits benefiting former client Securus
ajit_pai.post_fcc: Partner at Searchlight Capital Partners (private equity, April 2021); CEO/President of CTIA (April 1, 2025)
ajit_pai.revolving_door: From regulator to private equity to top wireless industry lobbyist

brendan_carr.pre_fcc: Attorney at Wiley Rein LLP (clients included Viacom, AOL Time Warner, Bell telephone companies)
brendan_carr.appointed_by: Trump (nominated 2017, renominated by Biden 2023, designated chair by Trump 2025)
brendan_carr.project_2025: Authored FCC chapter calling for regulating tech companies, transparency rules, and ending Section 230
brendan_carr.notable: First FCC chair to have written a Project 2025 chapter on the agency he leads

michael_powell.appointed_by: George W. Bush (originally nominated by Clinton to FCC in 1997)
michael_powell.post_fcc: President/CEO of NCTA (cable industry trade association) since April 25, 2011
michael_powell.revolving_door: Now lobbies for the same companies he once regulated; NCTA represents 90%+ of cable households
michael_powell.notable: Holds the same position (NCTA CEO) that Tom Wheeler held before becoming FCC Chair

meredith_attwell_baker.key_vote: Voted to approve Comcast-NBCUniversal merger January 2011
meredith_attwell_baker.post_fcc: Hired by Comcast as SVP of Government Affairs (June 2011) — just 4 months after approving merger
meredith_attwell_baker.revolving_door: Textbook case — approved mega-merger then immediately joined the merged company

c_band_auction_107.spectrum: 3.7-3.98 GHz (280 MHz of mid-band spectrum for 5G)
c_band_auction_107.status: Most successful spectrum auction in FCC history

current_battle.cable_vs_wireless: CTIA launched 'End the Cableopoly' campaign accusing cable of starving wireless providers of spectrum
current_battle.fcc_authority_restored: One Big Beautiful Bill restores FCC auction authority; sets $85B minimum target
current_battle.upper_c_band: FCC voted unanimously on rules for 100MHz+ upper C-band auction; deadline July 2027

comcast_nbcuniversal_2011.internet_essentials: Required to offer $9.95/month broadband to low-income families
comcast_nbcuniversal_2011.controversy: Commissioner Baker approved merger then joined Comcast 4 months later

att_time_warner_2018.ruling: AT&T won — no restrictions; decided by Judge Richard Leon (March-June 2018)
att_time_warner_2018.political: Trump opposed deal publicly citing CNN coverage; Wall Street suspected political motivation

disney_fox_2019.doj_condition: Required divestiture of 22 regional sports networks
disney_fox_2019.political: Trump congratulated Murdoch while attacking AT&T-Time Warner over CNN

t_mobile_sprint_2020.trump_hotel: T-Mobile executives spent $195,000 at Trump's DC hotel during merger review

telecommunications_act_1996.stated_goal: Let anyone enter any communications business to compete in any market
telecommunications_act_1996.actual_effect: Massive consolidation across radio, TV, and telecom

radio_impact.ownership_cap: Eliminated national ownership cap on commercial radio stations
radio_impact.clear_channel_growth: From 40 stations to 1,240 stations — 30x more than previously allowed
radio_impact.industry_consolidation: Radio station owners dropped from 5,100 to 3,800 within 5 years
radio_impact.lobbyist: Clear Channel founder Lowry Mays lobbied Senator Bob Dole to get ownership deregulation into the Act

sinclair_broadcasting.stations: Second largest TV station owner in US
sinclair_broadcasting.political_lean: 97% of executive contributions went to Republicans (2004); 80% of PAC money to Republicans (2017-2018)
sinclair_broadcasting.lobbying_2025: $800,000 (4x 2023 level focused on FCC ownership rules)
sinclair_broadcasting.hostile_takeover: Announced pursuit of hostile takeover of Scripps (November 2025)
sinclair_broadcasting.political_controversy: Forced local anchors to read scripted 'must-run' segments; FCC under Trump relaxed ownership rules benefiting Sinclair

nexstar.status: Largest TV station owner in US
nexstar.lobbyist_hired: Jeff Miller — Trump's second inaugural committee finance chair — paid $510,000 in 2025
nexstar.ownership_issue: Combined reach would exceed 39% national audience cap

iheart_media.conservative_programming: Carries Glenn Beck, Sean Hannity, Mark Levin, Clay Travis & Buck Sexton via Premiere Networks
iheart_media.ted_cruz_controversy: Investigated for in-kind marketing 'gift potentially worth millions' to Cruz; Super PAC received hundreds of thousands from iHeart

local_news_deserts.single_source_counties: 1,524 counties with only one remaining news source
local_news_deserts.americans_affected: 50M Americans with limited or no access to local news
local_news_deserts.newspapers_closed_2025: 136 in most recent year (2+ per week)
local_news_deserts.total_newspapers_lost: Almost 40% of all local US newspapers have vanished
local_news_deserts.digital_startups: 300+ local news startups in past 5 years (80% digital-only); less than 10% in rural counties

bead_program.purpose: Grants to 56 states/territories to bridge digital divide
bead_program.all_proposals_approved: November 19, 2024 — all 56 states/territories had initial proposals approved
bead_program.2025_rule_change: June 2025 — NTIA rescinded previous guidance; gave states 90 days to integrate new requirements

industry_lobbying.fiber_vs_alternatives: Industry lobbied against NTIA's prioritization of fiber; pushed for satellite and fixed wireless eligibility
industry_lobbying.price_caps_opposition: AT&T, Comcast, Charter, Verizon lobbied against rules requiring lower prices for low-income customers in exchange for BEAD funding
industry_lobbying.combined_lobbying: $117M in federal + $25M+ in state-level lobbying (2023) to influence infrastructure law implementation
industry_lobbying.ustelecom_position: Opposed rate regulation; called it 'a formula that will not allow flexibility'

affordable_connectivity_program.ended: June 1, 2024 — no additional funding from Congress
affordable_connectivity_program.peak_enrollment: 23M+ households receiving $30/month discount ($75 on tribal lands)
affordable_connectivity_program.industry_position: USTelecom and AT&T publicly supported extension; industry pointed to ACP as preferred solution for digital divide
affordable_connectivity_program.political_failure: Congress unable to pass extension despite bipartisan support

e_rate.annual_spend: ~$2B typically; $3.26B in discounts to schools in 2024
e_rate.purpose: Discounts (20-90%) on broadband for K-12 schools and public libraries
e_rate.controversy: Mobile carriers opposed expansion; smaller carriers opposed subsidized competition

broadband_definition_lobbying.2015_standard: 25 Mbps download / 3 Mbps upload (set by FCC)
broadband_definition_lobbying.2024_update: 100 Mbps download / 20 Mbps upload (FCC Chairwoman Rosenworcel)
broadband_definition_lobbying.cable_industry_influence: Upload speed of 20 Mbps was political compromise — cable industry lobbied hard against higher upload requirement
broadband_definition_lobbying.significance: Definition determines which areas qualify for federal funding; lower definition = fewer eligible areas = less competition for incumbents

sector_overview.historical_party_split: Roughly 51% to Democrats since 1990 cycle (slight Democratic lean)

individual_company_contributions_2024_cy.charter: 49% of PAC donations went to Democrats (among highest D-lean in telecom)

donation_pauses.january_6_response: AT&T, Verizon, and Comcast temporarily halted donations to lawmakers who opposed certifying Biden's 2020 election results

lobbying_vs_contributions.lobbying_dwarfs_pacs: Industry spends ~10x more on lobbying ($108M+) than direct campaign contributions ($10-15M)
lobbying_vs_contributions.state_level: $25M+ annually in state-level lobbying (not captured in federal figures)

current_status.bipartisan_sunset_bill: Graham-Durbin bill would sunset Section 230 on January 1, 2027 unless Congress enacts replacement
current_status.house_version: Energy and Commerce Committee draft to sunset by December 1, 2025
current_status.late_2025_bill: Graham, Durbin, Hawley, Klobuchar, Blackburn, and AG Ashley Moody unveiled bill (December 2025)
current_status.none_passed: No Section 230 reform bill has become law despite dozens introduced

big_tech_lobbying_to_preserve.five_year_total: $260M from 2020-2024 (Meta, Alphabet, Microsoft, ByteDance, X, Snap combined)
big_tech_lobbying_to_preserve.tactic: Public claims of supporting reform while lobbyists fight behind the scenes

telecom_vs_tech_positions.telecom_position: Generally supports Section 230 reform; Brendan Carr's Project 2025 chapter called for ending Section 230
telecom_vs_tech_positions.tech_position: Lobbies to preserve Section 230 protections while publicly expressing openness to reform
telecom_vs_tech_positions.divergence: Telecoms want tech platforms held to similar regulatory standards they face; tech companies resist being treated like common carriers

municipal_broadband_restrictions.states_with_bans_2024: 16 states still ban or restrict community-owned broadband networks

recent_rollbacks.arkansas_2021: Rolled back legislative barriers on publicly owned broadband
recent_rollbacks.washington_2021: Rolled back legislative barriers on publicly owned broadband
recent_rollbacks.colorado_2023: Repealed referendum requirement — 120+ communities had already voted to opt out

alec_model_legislation.bill_name: Municipal Telecommunications Private Industry Safeguards Act
alec_model_legislation.purpose: Model bill for states to block local governments from building public broadband
alec_model_legislation.north_carolina: 6 provisions in NC law nearly identical to ALEC model
alec_model_legislation.wilson_nc: Town's municipal fiber network specifically targeted by ALEC-backed legislation

types_of_restrictions.population_limits: Nevada — only municipalities under 25,000 and counties under 55,000 can offer telecom services
types_of_restrictions.utility_requirements: Arkansas, Tennessee — bar municipalities without electric utilities from providing internet
types_of_restrictions.service_limits: Missouri, Texas — municipal networks limited to internet only, no other telecom services
types_of_restrictions.unserved_only: Montana, Pennsylvania — permit municipal networks only in unserved communities
types_of_restrictions.cost_imputation: Virginia, Florida, South Carolina — require imputing private sector costs, paying additional taxes, setting excessively high prices

fcc_preemption_attempt.action: FCC attempted to preempt state laws restricting municipal broadband
fcc_preemption_attempt.outcome: Failed — courts ruled FCC lacks authority to reallocate power between states and municipalities

bead_interference.issue: State preemption laws interfere with BEAD program deployment — municipalities in restricted states cannot apply for federal broadband grants
bead_interference.chilling_effect: Even in states without outright bans, threat of preemption discourages municipal broadband planning

industry_scale.combined_lobbying_vs_revenue: Top 4 ISPs spend ~$50M on federal lobbying — roughly 0.01% of revenue; generates billions in regulatory advantages

regulatory_asymmetry.ratio: Industry spends roughly 28 cents in lobbying for every $1 of FCC budget

consumer_impact.competition: Most Americans have 1-2 choices for high-speed broadband; ~50M have 0-1 options
consumer_impact.acp_loss_impact: 23M households lost $30/month subsidy when ACP ended June 2024

historical_context.telecom_act_1996_lobbying: Telecom companies spent heavily to shape the Telecommunications Act of 1996; Clear Channel founder lobbied Senator Dole directly
historical_context.net_neutrality_decade: 2008-2017: $572M spent by top ISPs lobbying FCC on broadband regulation
historical_context.broadband_gatekeepers_report: Common Cause/CWA 2021 report found ISPs used political spending to block Save the Internet Act, Accessible Affordable Internet for All Act, and RESILIENT Networks Act
historical_context.trump_era_1: Pai FCC: repealed net neutrality, relaxed media ownership rules, approved T-Mobile-Sprint merger
historical_context.biden_era: Rosenworcel FCC: attempted net neutrality restoration (struck down), updated broadband definition, launched ACP and BEAD
historical_context.trump_era_2: Carr FCC: net neutrality dead after Sixth Circuit ruling; E-Rate rollback; media ownership deregulation expected; BEAD rules rescinded and rewritten
